historical_cases.name: Post-Three Mile Island Nuclear Rehabilitation (Late 1990s–2000s)
historical_cases.peak_move: Uranium spot prices rose from under $10/lb to over $130/lb by 2007; nuclear utility stocks and uranium miners saw multi-hundred-percent gains across the cycle
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Fukushima Daiichi disaster (March 2011) abruptly reversed sentiment; Germany announced phase-out, Japan shuttered fleet, uranium prices collapsed over 70% from peak

historical_cases.name: 2006–2008 Uranium Bull Market / 'Nuclear Renaissance' Narrative
historical_cases.peak_move: Uranium spot surged from ~$20 to ~$136/lb; Cameco, Paladin Energy, and uranium ETFs saw explosive appreciation; reactor orderbooks expanded significantly
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Combination of financial crisis demand destruction (2008) and subsequent Fukushima shock (2011) permanently ended that cycle; planned reactor builds were cancelled or deferred en masse

historical_cases.name: Post-Fukushima Recovery & Carbon-Driven Nuclear Re-evaluation (2018–2021)
historical_cases.peak_move: Gradual re-rating of existing nuclear operators; uranium physical funds (e.g., Sprott) began accumulating; modest but sustained re-rating of nuclear equities
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Theme did not fully exit but stalled on lack of concrete new-build catalysts and persistent regulatory friction; momentum folded into the broader 2022 energy crisis re-acceleration

historical_cases.name: 2022 European Energy Crisis Nuclear Re-Rating
historical_cases.peak_move: EDF and European nuclear utilities surged; Belgium reversed phase-out; uranium spot re-accelerated above $50/lb; political consensus in EU began shifting toward nuclear taxonomy inclusion
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Acute energy price normalization through mid-2023 reduced urgency premium; theme evolved into slower-burn SMR/supply chain narrative rather than collapsing outright

differences.description: AI data center power demand has created an entirely new category of baseload electricity buyer with explicit nuclear procurement commitments — Microsoft-Constellation (Three Mile Island restart), Google-Kairos, Amazon-X-energy deals represent demand-pull that was absent in all prior nuclear cycles. Hyperscalers are signing 20-year PPAs specifically for nuclear output, providing revenue visibility that historically never existed for prospective new builds.

SMR designs (NuScale, GE-Hitachi BWRX-300, Rolls-Royce SMR, Oklo, TerraPower) represent a genuine paradigm shift from the gigawatt-scale overnight-cost overruns that killed prior cycles. Modular factory fabrication aims to break the cost-escalation pathology historically endemic to nuclear construction, though no SMR has yet achieved commercial operation at scale — meaning the technology promise remains partially unvalidated.

Regulatory environment has materially shifted: U.S. ADVANCE Act (2024) streamlined NRC licensing; DOE loan guarantees and production tax credits under IRA provide explicit subsidy backstop; NATO-aligned governments are treating nuclear as energy security infrastructure rather than purely an environmental debate. This policy architecture was not present in the 2006–2008 or 2018–2021 cycles.

Uranium enrichment and fuel fabrication supply chains remain structurally constrained — Western enrichment capacity (Urenco, Centrus) is limited, and post-Russia sanctions on TENEX have exposed concentration risk. Unlike prior cycles where supply could respond within a few years, HALEU (high-assay low-enriched uranium) production for advanced reactors faces a multi-year ramp with few qualified producers, potentially sustaining fuel price elevation longer than historical analogs.

similarities: Sentiment cycles in nuclear have historically been highly binary — euphoria followed by a single catastrophic event (Chernobyl, Fukushima) reset decades of progress; tail-risk from any operational incident anywhere in the world remains the dominant exogenous exit risk, as observed repeatedly

Construction cost and schedule overruns have plagued every Western nuclear new-build attempt in the modern era (Vogtle, Hinkley Point C, Flamanville); until an SMR demonstrates on-time, on-budget delivery, investor skepticism from prior cycles may periodically resurface as a valuation ceiling

The gap between announced project pipelines and actual grid-connected capacity has historically been very wide; prior 'nuclear renaissance' narratives (2006–2011) also featured large orderbooks that were subsequently cancelled, suggesting the current announced SMR pipeline should be discounted for execution risk
this_time_different.observation: The current nuclear renaissance may be structurally more durable than its predecessors due to the convergence of AI-driven baseload demand and policy support, but the cycle's ultimate length remains dependent on whether even one SMR platform achieves commercial-scale operation without the cost and schedule pathologies that terminated prior cycles.

exit_signals: A significant nuclear operational incident (even outside the U.S./Europe) triggers regulatory freezes, PPA renegotiations, or hyperscaler counterparty withdrawals from nuclear procurement commitments — historically the single most reliable cycle-ender

First commercial SMR project (NuScale UAMPS, BWRX-300 Ontario, or equivalent) announces material cost overrun or cancellation, validating the 'same story different decade' bear thesis and removing the technology differentiation premium from SMR equities

AI data center power demand growth decelerates sharply or hyperscalers pivot to alternative baseload solutions (geothermal at scale, long-duration storage breakthroughs), removing the demand-pull anchor that differentiates this cycle

Uranium spot and term prices retreat sustainably below ~$65/lb, signaling that the physical market supply-demand narrative has loosened and removing the fuel economics underpinning upstream miner and enricher valuations

Broad risk-off macro environment or credit tightening raises the cost of capital for infrastructure-duration nuclear projects to levels that make projected returns uneconomic, as observed during the 2008 financial crisis which stalled the prior renaissance
duration_type_reasoning: SMR commercialization is a multi-year infrastructure deployment cycle with no inherent expiration date — progress is gated by regulatory approvals, construction milestones, and grid integration timelines measured in years to decades, making it structurally extended rather than bounded or derivative.

real_world_timeline.description: The current SMR-focused nuclear renaissance phase accelerated in mid-to-late 2023 when Constellation's Three Mile Island restart announcement and a wave of hyperscaler nuclear PPAs transformed nuclear from a policy debate into an active commercial procurement story, coinciding with uranium spot prices breaking above multi-year resistance levels.
